macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishna and Its Cryptic Language: Assessment and Payment of Damages

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks on the archaic language and early date of the Mishna
The Mishna text on assessment of money and payment of damages

macro_segments.title: Shum Kesef and the Case of Mutual Damage: The Para and Talit Dispute

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda's interpretation: Assessment must be in terms of monetary value
The case of the cow and garment: Why we measure in money, not ownership
Tosafot's two chidushim: No automatic ownership of the damaging animal; no lien on the damaged object
Discussion of Rabbi Akiva's view that the injured party becomes automatic part-owner of the damaging animal

macro_segments.title: Shaveh Kesef: Payment in Property with Monetary Value and the Braita's Reinterpretation

micro_segments.title: Initial understanding of shaveh kesef as payment in any chattel equivalent to money
The Braita's surprising interpretation: Shaveh kesef refers to land, not chattel
Rabba bar Ulla's explanation: Land as the property with no fixed price and no prohibition of overcharging
Rav Ashi's alternative: Shaveh kesef means something like money but not money itself; all chattel is considered money

macro_segments.title: Bifnei Beis Din: Court Authority, Witnesses, and the Exclusion of Non-Qualified Judges

micro_segments.title: The Braita's requirement that assessment and payment happen before a proper Beis Din
Why Bifnei Beis Din excludes commoner courts without semicha authority
Al pi eidim: The requirement for valid witnesses and the exclusion of slaves and non-Jews

macro_segments.title: Exclusion from Self-Admission in Cases of Fine: The Modeh Beknas Principle

micro_segments.title: The principle that self-admission cannot obligate payment of a fine
Why fines require objective testimony: They are punishments, not compensation
Debate over whether subsequent witnesses override the self-admitted fine

macro_segments.title: Bifnei Beis Din: Court Authority, Witnesses, and the Exclusion of Non-Qualified Judges (continued)

micro_segments.title: Why we need explicit mention of both free persons and members of the covenant

macro_segments.title: Vehanashim Beklal Hanezek: Women's Inclusion in Damages and the Hava Amina Challenges

micro_segments.title: The source that women are included in all laws of damages
Why Torah is written in masculine: Presumption that laws apply equally unless excluded
Multiple proofs of women's equal status in tort law from different verses
The disturbing hava amina that women might not be included based on fewer mitzvot obligations
The principle that women are not bar mitzvah and implications for atonement and damages
Tosafot's discussion of whether women can serve as judges despite being disqualified as witnesses

macro_segments.title: Hanizak Vehamazik Betashlumin: Both Parties Participate in Paying Damages

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the principle that both the injured and damager participate in payment

macro_segments.title: Palga Nizka: The Central Dispute Between Rav Papa and Rav Huna on Compensation vs. Fine

micro_segments.title: Rav Papa's position: Half damages is compensation for negligence in an unwatched animal
Rav Huna's position: Half damages is a fine to incentivize care of naturally unwatchful animals
Both positions agree on the borderline nature of the tam animal's liability

macro_segments.title: Proofs for Mamona vs. Knasa Classification: Mining Tannaitic Sources for Evidence

micro_segments.title: First proof attempt: The Mishna on differences between tam and muad does not mention modeh beknas

macro_segments.title: Hanizak Vehamazik Betashlumin: Both Parties Participate in Paying Damages (continued)

micro_segments.title: If half damages is compensation, the injured party essentially pays for half their own loss
If half damages is a fine, the injured party's loss of carcass value explains their participation
Teaching about depreciation of carcass appearing in an earlier mishna

macro_segments.title: Proofs for Mamona vs. Knasa Classification: Mining Tannaitic Sources for Evidence (continued)

micro_segments.title: Counter: The list may simply be incomplete; an opinion exists that tam pays half kofer
Second proof: A braita that one pays on self-admission when one admits to goring with a tam ox
The critical rule: Anything more than actual damage is a fine; exact amount or less is compensation
Resolving the rule: Sometimes paying less than full damage is compensation; not all underpayment is a fine
Example of compensation with partial payment: Half damages of tzerorot from indirect animal causation

macro_segments.title: Final Ruling: Half Damages by Tam is a Fine, Not Compensation

micro_segments.title: Despite textual ambiguity, the Gemara rules that chatzi nezek of a tam goring is a fine
The implications of this ruling will be explored further, particularly regarding payment sources

topical_tags.term: Bifnei Beis Din (In Front of Court)
Hanashim Beklal Hanezek (Women Included in Damages)
Hanizak Vehamazik Betashlumin (Both Parties in Payment)
Rabbi Akiva vs. Rabbi Yishmael Debate
Avid Inish Dina Lnafshei (Taking the Law Into One's Own Hands)
